The percentages are the overlaps between the testing and training negatives near convergence. 6.2 E MPIRICAL ANALYSES ON TRAINING CONVERGENCE We п¬Ғrst show the long tail distribution of search relevance in dense retrieval. As plotted in Fig. 3, there are a few instances per query with signiп¬Ғcant higher retrieval scores, while the majority form a long tail. In retrieval/ranking, the key challenge is to distinguish the relevant ones among those highest scored ones; the rest is trivially irrelevant. We also empirically measure the probability of local in-batch negatives including informative negatives (DвҲ’вҲ—), by their overlap with top 100 highest scored negatives. This probability, either using NCE Neg or Rand Neg, iszero, the same as our theory assumes. In comparison, the overlap between BM25 Neg with top DR retrieved negatives is 15%, while that of ANCE negatives starts at 63% and converges to 100% by design. Then we empirically validate our theory that local negatives lead to lower loss, bounded gradient norm, and thus slow convergence. The training loss and pre-clip gradient norms during DR training are plotted in Fig. 4. As expected, the uninformative local negatives are trivial to separate, yielding near-zero training loss, while ANCE global negatives are much harder and maintain a high training loss. The same with our theoretical assumption, the gradient norms of local negatives are indeed restricted closely to zero. In comparison, the gradient norms on ANCE global negatives are bigger by orders of magnitude. This conп¬Ғrms ANCE better approximates the oracle importance sampling distribution pвҲ— dвҲ’ вҲқ||вҲҮОёtl(d+,dвҲ’)||2 and improves learning convergence. 6.3 D ISCUSSIONS We use BERT-Siamese and NLL loss to be consistent with recent research. We have experimented with cosine similarity and BCE/hinge loss, where we observe even smaller gradient norms on local negatives. But the retrieval accuracy is not much better. We include additional experiments in Appendix. A.2 discusses the surprisingly small overlap (<25%) between dense retrieval results and sparse retrieval results. DR is a fundamentally different approach and more studies are required to understand its behavior. A.3 and A.4 study the asynchronous gaps and hyperparameters. The x-axes are training steps. 7 R ELATED WORK In early research on neural information retrieval (Neu-IR) (Mitra et al., 2018), a common belief was that the interaction models, those that speciп¬Ғcally handle term level matches, are more effective though more expensive (Guo et al., 2016; Xiong et al., 2017; Nogueira & Cho, 2019). Many techniques are developed to reduce their cost, for example, distillation (Gao et al., 2020a) and caching (Humeau et al., 2020; Khattab & Zaharia, 2020; MacAvaney et al., 2020). ANCE shows that a properly trained representation-based BERT-Siamese is in fact as effective as the interaction-based BERT ranker. This п¬Ғnding will motivate many new research explorations in Neu-IR. Deep learning has been used to improve various components of sparse retrieval, for example, term weighting (Dai & Callan, 2019b), query expansion (Zheng et al., 2020), and document expan- sion (Nogueira et al., 2019). Dense Retrieval chooses a different path and conducts retrieval purely in the embedding space via ANN search (Lee et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Luan et al., 2020). This work demonstrates that a simple dense retrieval system can achieve SOTA accuracy, while also behaves dramatically different from classic retrieval. The recent advancement in dense retrieval may raise a new generation of search systems. Recent research in contrastive representation learning also shows the beneп¬Ғts of sampling negatives from a larger candidate pool. In computer vision, He et al. (2019) decouple the negative sampling pool size with training batch size, by maintaining a negative candidate pool of recent batches and updating their representation with momentum. This enlarged negative pool signiп¬Ғcantly improves unsupervised visual representation learning (Chen et al., 2020b). A parallel work (Xiong et al., 2020) improves DPR by sampling negatives from a memory bank (Wu et al., 2018) вҖ” in which the representations of negative candidates are frozen so more candidates can be stored. Instead of a bigger local pool, ANCE goes all the way along this trajectory and constructs negatives globally from the entire corpus, using an asynchronously updated ANN index. Besides being a real world application itself, dense retrieval is also a core component in many other language systems, for example, to retrieval relevant information for grounded language models (Khan- delwal et al., 2019; Guu et al., 2020), extractive/generative QA (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020b), and fact veriп¬Ғcation (Xiong et al., 2020), or to п¬Ғnd paraphrase pairs for pretraining (Lewis et al., 2020a). There dense retrieval models are either frozen or optimized indirectly by signals from their end tasks. ANCE is orthogonal with those lines of research and focuses on the representation learning for dense retrieval. Its better retrieval accuracy can beneп¬Ғt many language systems. 8 C ONCLUSION In this paper, we п¬Ғrst provide theoretical analyses on the convergence of representation learning in dense retrieval. We show that under common conditions in text retrieval, the local negatives used in DR training are uninformative, yield low gradient norms, and contribute little to the learning convergence. We then propose ANCE to eliminate this bottleneck by constructing training negatives globally from the entire corpus. Our experiments demonstrate the advantage of ANCE in web search, OpenQA, and the production system of a commercial search engine. Our studies empirically validate our theory that ANCE negatives have much bigger gradient norms, reduce the stochastic gradient variance, and improve training convergence. 8Preprint REFERENCES Guillaume Alain, Alex Lamb, Chinnadhurai Sankar, Aaron Courville, and Yoshua Bengio. The training and development sets are from MS MARCO, which includes passage level relevance labels for one million Bing queries (Bajaj et al., 2016). The document corpus was post-constructed by back-п¬Ғlling the body texts of the passageвҖҷs URLs and their labels were inherited from its passages (Craswell et al., 2020). The testing sets are labeled by NIST accessors on the top 10 ranked results from past Track participants (Craswell et al., 2020). TREC DL ofп¬Ғcial metrics include NDCG@10 on test and MRR@10 on MARCO Passage Dev. MARCO Document Dev is noisy and the recall on the DL Track testing is less meaningful due to low label coverage on DR results. There is a two-year gap between the construction of the passage training data and the back-п¬Ғlling of their full document content. Some original documents were no longer available. There is also a decent amount of content changes in those documents during the two-year gap, and many no longer contain the passages. This back-п¬Ғlling perhaps is the reason why many Track participants found the passage training data is more effective than the inherited document labels. Note that the TREC testing labels are not inп¬Ӯuenced as the annotators were provided the same document contents when judging. All the TREC DL runs are trained using these training data. Their inference results on the testing queries of the document and the passage retrieval tasks were evaluated by NIST assessors in the standard TREC-style pooling technique (V oorhees, 2000). The pooling depth is set to 10, that is, the top 10 ranked results from all participated runs are evaluated, and these evaluated labels are released as the ofп¬Ғcial TREC DL benchmarks for passage and document retrieval tasks. More Details on OpenQA Experiments: All the DPR related experimental settings, baseline systems, and DPR Reader are based on their open source libarary1. The RAG-Token reader uses their open-source release in huggingface2. The RAG-Seq release in huggingface is not yet stable by the time we did our experiment, thus we choose the RAG-Token in our OpenQA experiment. RAG only releases the NQ models thus we use DPR reader on TriviaQA. We feed top 20 passages from ANCE to RAG-Token on NQ and top 100 passages to DPRвҖҷs BERT Reader, following the guideline in their open-source codes. More Details on Baselines: The most representative sparse retrieval baselines in TREC DL include the standard BM25 (вҖңbm25baseвҖқ or вҖңbm25base_pвҖқ), Best TREC Sparse Retrieval (вҖңbm25tuned_rm3вҖқ or вҖңbm25tuned_prf_pвҖқ) with tuned query expansion (Lavrenko & Croft, 2017), and Best DeepCT (вҖңdct_tp_bm25e2вҖқ, doc only), which uses BERT to estimate the term importance for BM25 (Dai & Callan, 2019a). These three runs represent the standard sparse retrieval, best classical sparse retrieval, and the recent progress of using BERT to im- prove sparse retrieval. We also include the standard cascade retrieval-and-reranking systems BERT Reranker (вҖңbm25exp_marcombвҖқ or вҖңp_exp_rm3_bertвҖқ), which is the best run using standard BERT on top of query/doc expansion, from the groups with multiple top MARCO runs (Nogueira & Cho, 2019; Nogueira et al., 2019). BERT-Siamese Conп¬Ғgurations: We follow the network conп¬Ғgurations in Luan et al. (2020) in all Dense Retrieval methods, which we found provides the most stable results. More speciп¬Ғcally, we initialize the BERT- Siamese model with RoBERTa base (Liu et al., 2019) and add a768 Г—768 projection layer on top of the last layerвҖҷs вҖң[CLS]вҖқ token, followed by a layer norm. Implementation Details: The training often takes about 1-2 hours per ANCE epoch, which is whenever new ANCE negative is ready, it immediately replaces existing negatives in training, without waiting. It converges in about 10 epochs, similar to other DR baselines. The optimization uses LAMB optimizer, learning rate 5e-6 for document and 1e-6 for passage retrieval, and linear warm-up and decay after 5000 steps. More detailed hyperparameter settings can be found in our code release. 1https://github.com/facebookresearch/DPR. 2https://huggingface.co/transformers/master/model_doc/rag.html 12Preprint 0 100 2000.2 0.5 0.8 1.1 1.4Loss NDCG@10 Loss (a) 10k Batch; 4:4; 1e-5 0 100 200 (b) 20k Batch; 8:4; 1e-6 0 100 200 (c) 5k Batch; 4:8; 1e-6 0 100 200 0.4 0.5 0.6 NDCG@10 (d) 10k Batch; 4:4; 5e-6 Figure 5: Training loss and testing NDCG of ANCE (FirstP) on documents, with different ANN index refreshing (e.g., per 10k Batch), Trainer:Inferencer GPU allocation, and learning rate (e.g., 1e-5). X-axes is the training steps in thousands. Table 7: Results of several different hyperparameter conп¬Ғgurations. вҖңTop K NegвҖқ lists the top k ANN retrieved candidates from which we sampled the ANCE negatives from. Hyperparameter MARCO Dev PassageTREC DL Document Learning rate Top K Neg Refresh (step) Retrieval MRR@10 Retrieval NDCG@10 Passage ANCE 1e-6 200 10k 0.33 вҖ“ 1e-6 500 10k 0.31 вҖ“ 2e-6 200 10k 0.29 вҖ“ 2e-7 500 20k 0.303 вҖ“ 2e-7 1000 20k 0.302 вҖ“ Document ANCE 1e-5 100 10k вҖ“ 0.58 1e-6 100 20k вҖ“ 0.59 1e-6 100 5k вҖ“ 0.60 5e-6 200 10k вҖ“ 0.614 1e-6 200 10k вҖ“ 0.61 A.2 O VERLAP WITH SPARSE RETRIEVAL IN TREC 2019 DL T RACK As a nature of TREC-style pooling evaluation, only those ranked in the top 10 by the 2019 TREC participating systems were labeled. As a result, documents not in the pool and thus not labeled are all considered irrelevant, even though there may be relevant ones among them. When reusing TREC style relevance labels, it is very important to keep track of the вҖңhole rateвҖқ on the evaluated systems, i.e., the fraction of the top K ranked results without TREC labels (not in the pool). A larger hole rate shows that the evaluated methods are very different from those systems that participated in the Track and contributed to the pool, thus the evaluation results are not perfect. Note that the hole rate does not necessarily reп¬Ӯect the accuracy of the system, only the difference of it. In TREC 2019 Deep Learning Track, all the participating systems are based on sparse retrieval. Dense retrieval methods often differ considerably from sparse retrievals and in general will retrieve many new documents. This is conп¬Ғrmed in Table 6. All DR methods have very low overlap with the ofп¬Ғcial BM25 in their top 100 retrieved documents. At most, only 25% of documents retrieved by DR are also retrieved by BM25. This makes the hole rate quite high and the recall metric not very informative. It also suggests that DR methods might beneп¬Ғt more in this yearвҖҷs TREC 2020 Deep Learning Track if participants are contributing DR based systems. The MS MARCO ranking labels were not constructed based on pooling the sparse retrieval results. They were from Bing (Bajaj et al., 2016), which uses many signals beyond term overlap. This makes the recall metric in MS MARCO more robust as it reп¬Ӯects how a single model can recover a complex online system. A.3 I MPACT OF ASYNCHRONOUS GAP Fig. 5 illustrates the behavior of asynchronous learning with different conп¬Ғgurations. A large learning rate or a low refreshing rate (Figure 5(a) and 5(b)) leads to п¬Ӯuctuations as the async gap of the ANN index may drive the representation learning to undesired local optima. Refreshing as often as every 5k Batches yields a smooth convergence (Figure 5(c)), but requires twice as many GPU allocated to the Inferencer. A 1:1 GPUs allocation of Trainer and Inference with appropriate learning rates is adequate to minimize the impact of async gap. 13Preprint Table 8: Queries in the TREC 2019 DL Track Document Ranking Tasks where ANCE performs better than BM25. The cost of training with BERT makes it difп¬Ғcult to conduct a lot hyperparameter exploration. Often a failed conп¬Ғguration leads to divergence early in training. We barely explore other conп¬Ғgurations due to the time-consuming nature of working with pretrained language models. Our DR model architecture is kept consistent with recent parallel work and the learning conп¬Ғgurations in Table 7 are about all the explorations we did. Most of the hyperparameter choices are decided solely using the training loss curve and otherwise by the loss in the MARCO Dev set. We found the training loss, validation NDCG, and testing performance align well in our (limited) hyperparameter explorations. A.5 C ASE STUDIES In this section, we show Win/Loss case studies between ANCE and BM25. Among the 43 TREC 2019 DL Track evaluation queries in the document task, ANCE outperforms BM25 on 29 queries, loses on 13 queries, and ties on the rest 1 query. The winning examples are shown in Table 8 and the losing ones are in Table 9. Their corresponding ANCE-learned (FirstP) representations are illustrated by t-SNE in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7. In general, we found ANCE better captures the semantics in the documents and their relevance to the query. The winning cases show the intrinsic limitations of sparse retrieval. For example, BM25 exact matches the вҖңmost popular foodвҖқ in the query вҖңwhat is the most popular food in SwitzerlandвҖқ but using the document is about Mexico. Many times we found that it is not that DR fails completely and retrieves documents not related to the queryвҖҷs information needs at all, which was a big concern when we started research in DR. The errors ANCE made include retrieving documents that are related just not exactly relevant to the query, for example, вҖңyoga poseвҖқ for вҖңbow in yogaвҖқ. In other cases, ANCE retrieved wrong documents due to the lack of the domain knowledge: the pretrained language model may not know вҖңactive 15Preprint (a) 182539: monotonic function  (b) 1117099: active margin Query Relevant ANCE Neg BM25 Neg Rand Neg (c) 1132213: yoga bow Figure 7: t-SNE Plots for Losing Cases in Table 9. marginвҖқ is a geographical terminology, not a п¬Ғnancial one (which we did not know ourselves and took some time to п¬Ғgure out when conducting this case study). There are also some cases where the dense retrieved documents make sense to us but were labeled irrelevant. The t-SNE plots in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 show many interesting patterns of the learned representation space. The ANCE winning cases often correspond to clear separations of different document groups. For losing cases the representation space is more mixed, or there is too few relevant documents which may cause the variances in model performances. There are also many different interesting patterns in the ANCE-learned representation space. We include the t-SNE plots for all 43 TREC DL Track queries in the supplementary material. More future analyses of the learned patterns in the representation space may help provide more insights on dense retrieval. 16

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper identifies that the primary bottleneck for dense retrieval (DR) models is the use of uninformative, locally-sampled negative examples during training, which leads to diminishing gradients and slow convergence. The main contribution is a new training mechanism called Approximate nearest neighbor Negative Contrastive Learning (ANCE), which selects hard negative examples globally from the entire corpus using an asynchronously updated Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) index. This approach aligns the training distribution with the test-time distribution of negatives, significantly improving DR performance to nearly match complex cascade models while being over 100 times more efficient.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: ANCE uses a dual-encoder (BERT-Siamese) architecture with a dot product similarity function. The core of the methodology is its negative sampling strategy: it leverages the DR model being trained to retrieve the top-k most similar (i.e., hardest) documents from an ANN index of the entire corpus. To manage the computational cost of keeping the index up-to-date, it employs an asynchronous refresh mechanism. A 'Trainer' process learns the model using the current index, while a parallel 'Inferencer' process uses a recent model checkpoint to re-encode the corpus and periodically update the ANN index for the Trainer.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated on three distinct tasks: 1) Web Search, using the TREC 2019 Deep Learning Track benchmark with MS MARCO for training data, evaluated on NDCG@10 and MRR@10. 2) Open Domain Question Answering (OpenQA), using the Natural Questions (NQ) and TriviaQA (TQA) datasets, evaluated on answer coverage. 3) A commercial search engine's production system. Baselines included sparse methods (BM25, DeepCT), cascade models (BERT Reranker), and other dense retrieval models with different negative sampling strategies (in-batch random, in-batch hard negatives (NCE), and BM25 negatives).
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The primary limitation is the 'asynchronous gap' between the model used for training and the one used for negative sampling, which can cause training instability if the index refresh rate is too low or the learning rate is too high. The evaluation on TREC benchmarks suffers from a high 'hole rate' because DR methods retrieve many documents not present in the original relevance pools, which were created from sparse retrieval systems. The model can also fail due to a lack of specific domain knowledge in the pre-trained language model, as shown in case studies.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: The paper suggests that its findings will motivate further research into representation-based models, potentially shifting focus away from more expensive interaction-based models. It also highlights the need for more in-depth studies to understand the fundamentally different retrieval behaviors of dense vs. sparse models. Further analysis of the learned patterns in the embedding space could provide deeper insights. Finally, since ANCE improves the core retrieval component, its application could benefit a wide range of downstream knowledge-intensive NLP tasks like complex QA and fact verification.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
The training process is orchestrated by the `BiEncoderTrainer` class in `train_dense_encoder.py`. The core logic for a training epoch is within the `_train_epoch` method, which iterates over data, creates batches, and performs forward/backward passes.

File: `train_dense_encoder.py`
```python
def _train_epoch(
    self,
    scheduler,
    epoch: int,
    eval_step: int,
    train_data_iterator: MultiSetDataIterator,
):
    # ... (setup)
    for i, samples_batch in enumerate(train_data_iterator.iterate_ds_data(epoch=epoch)):
        # ... (get dataset specific config)

        biencoder_batch = biencoder.create_biencoder_input(
            samples_batch,
            self.tensorizer,
            True,
            num_hard_negatives,
            num_other_negatives,
            shuffle=True,
            shuffle_positives=shuffle_positives,
            query_token=special_token,
        )

        # ... (get representation positions)

        loss, correct_cnt = _do_biencoder_fwd_pass(
            self.biencoder,
            biencoder_batch,
            self.tensorizer,
            cfg,
            encoder_type=encoder_type,
            rep_positions=rep_positions,
            loss_scale=loss_scale,
        )

        # ... (loss backward and optimizer step)
```

The `create_biencoder_input` method in `dpr/models/biencoder.py` assembles batches with positive passages and hard negatives mined in previous steps.

File: `dpr/models/biencoder.py`
```python
def create_biencoder_input(
    self,
    samples: List[BiEncoderSample],
    # ... (other params)
) -> BiEncoderBatch:
    # ...
    for sample in samples:
        # ...
        positive_ctx = sample.positive_passages[0]

        neg_ctxs = sample.negative_passages
        hard_neg_ctxs = sample.hard_negative_passages
        # ... (shuffling)

        if hard_neg_fallback and len(hard_neg_ctxs) == 0:
            hard_neg_ctxs = neg_ctxs[0:num_hard_negatives]

        neg_ctxs = neg_ctxs[0:num_other_negatives]
        hard_neg_ctxs = hard_neg_ctxs[0:num_hard_negatives]

        all_ctxs = [positive_ctx] + neg_ctxs + hard_neg_ctxs
        # ... (tensor creation)
    # ...
    return BiEncoderBatch(...)
```

The forward pass and loss calculation, which uses in-batch negatives (and gathers them across devices in distributed training), are handled by `_do_biencoder_fwd_pass` and `_calc_loss`.

File: `train_dense_encoder.py`
```python
def _do_biencoder_fwd_pass(
    model: nn.Module,
    input: BiEncoderBatch,
    tensorizer: Tensorizer,
    cfg,
    # ...
) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, int]:
    # ...
    model_out = model(
        # ... (input tensors)
    )
    local_q_vector, local_ctx_vectors = model_out
    loss_function = BiEncoderNllLoss()
    loss, is_correct = _calc_loss(
        cfg,
        loss_function,
        local_q_vector,
        local_ctx_vectors,
        input.is_positive,
        input.hard_negatives,
        loss_scale=loss_scale,
    )
    # ...
    return loss, is_correct
```

The loss function uses dot product similarity for scores.

File: `dpr/models/biencoder.py`
```python
class BiEncoderNllLoss(object):
    def calc(
        self,
        q_vectors: T,
        ctx_vectors: T,
        positive_idx_per_question: list,
        # ...
    ) -> Tuple[T, int]:
        scores = self.get_scores(q_vectors, ctx_vectors)
        # ...
        softmax_scores = F.log_softmax(scores, dim=1)
        loss = F.nll_loss(
            softmax_scores,
            torch.tensor(positive_idx_per_question).to(softmax_scores.device),
            reduction="mean",
        )
        # ...
        return loss, correct_predictions_count

    @staticmethod
    def get_scores(q_vector: T, ctx_vectors: T) -> T:
        f = BiEncoderNllLoss.get_similarity_function()
        return f(q_vector, ctx_vectors)

    @staticmethod
    def get_similarity_function():
        return dot_product_scores
```

The asynchronous 'Inferencer' process is implemented in `generate_dense_embeddings.py`, which uses a model checkpoint to encode the corpus.

File: `generate_dense_embeddings.py`
```python
def gen_ctx_vectors(
    # ...
) -> List[Tuple[object, np.array]]:
    # ...
    for j, batch_start in enumerate(range(0, n, bsz)):
        # ... (prepare batch tensors)
        with torch.no_grad():
            _, out, _ = model(ctx_ids_batch, ctx_seg_batch, ctx_attn_mask)
        out = out.cpu()
        # ... (store results)
    return results
```
Finally, `dense_retriever.py` is used to build the ANN index from the generated embeddings and then retrieve the top-k documents, which become the hard negatives for the next training cycle.

File: `dense_retriever.py`
```python
class LocalFaissRetriever(DenseRetriever):
    # ...
    def index_encoded_data(
        self,
        vector_files: List[str],
        # ...
    ):
        # ...
        for i, item in enumerate(iterate_encoded_files(vector_files, path_id_prefixes=path_id_prefixes)):
            buffer.append(item)
            if 0 < buffer_size == len(buffer):
                self.index.index_data(buffer)
                buffer = []
        self.index.index_data(buffer)

    def get_top_docs(self, query_vectors: np.array, top_docs: int = 100) -> List[Tuple[List[object], List[float]]]:
        # ...
        results = self.index.search_knn(query_vectors, top_docs)
        # ...
        return results
```
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
The ANCE method uses a dual-encoder architecture implemented in `dpr.models.biencoder.BiEncoder`, consisting of two BERT models (question and context encoders) that are not weight-shared. The similarity between question and context embeddings is calculated using dot product, as seen in `dpr.models.biencoder.dot_product_scores`.

The training objective is the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) of the correct passage, computed over a set of in-batch negatives (`BiEncoderNllLoss`). In a distributed training setting, embeddings are gathered from all processes to construct a larger, global batch of negatives for loss calculation (`_calc_loss` in `train_dense_encoder.py`).

The core of the methodology is an asynchronous negative sampling pipeline:
1.  **Trainer**: The main training script, `train_dense_encoder.py`, consumes training data containing positive passages and hard negative passages that were mined in a previous iteration.
2.  **Inferencer**: A separate script, `generate_dense_embeddings.py`, takes a recent checkpoint from the trainer and re-encodes the entire document corpus into dense vectors.
3.  **Indexer & Miner**: The `dense_retriever.py` script is used first to build an ANN (FAISS) index from the newly generated document vectors. Then, the same script is used to retrieve the top-k most similar (i.e., hardest) documents for each training question. These retrieved documents are then used to construct the hard negatives for the next cycle of the Trainer. This asynchronous refresh mechanism allows the model to be trained on progressively harder negatives generated by a stronger version of itself, without the prohibitive cost of updating the index synchronously within the training loop.

research_study_list.title: Efficient Training of Retrieval Models using Negative Cache

new_method.method:
### Momentum-Based Trajectory Debiasing for Contrastive Learning

**Motivation:**

Contrastive learning's success with hard negatives is often undermined by "false negatives"вҖ”semantically similar instances that are incorrectly repelled. Existing solutions are insufficient. Debiased Contrastive Learning (Chuang et al.) employs a static, global class prior (`tau_plus`) that cannot adapt to the local structure of the evolving embedding space. Other methods, like SRNS, use score variance as a proxy for reliability, but this signal is ambiguous: a stable, true hard negative on the decision boundary can exhibit low variance, appearing identical to a false negative. We argue that a more direct and robust signal is the temporal *trajectory* of similarity scores. As a model learns, a false negative's representation converges toward its anchor, causing their similarity score to show a consistently positive gradient. Conversely, a true hard negative is pushed away or remains at a stable high-similarity boundary. We propose a method that leverages a novel, state-free proxy for this gradient to perform self-correcting debiasing without requiring class priors or storing historical data.

**Methodology:**

We introduce a dynamic debiasing mechanism integrated into a momentum-based contrastive framework (e.g., MoCo) that is computationally efficient and requires no auxiliary state storage.

1.  **Dual-Encoder Signal Extraction:** Our method leverages the inherent temporal gap between the online encoder (`f_q`) and the slower-moving momentum encoder (`f_k`). For a given query image `x_q`, we compute two representations: the online query `q_online = f_q(x_q)` and the momentum query `q_momentum = f_k(x_q)`. The negative keys `{k_i}` in the memory queue are embeddings from the momentum encoder, i.e., `k_i = f_k(x_i)`.

2.  **Momentum-based Trajectory Score (MTS) for False Negative Identification:** For a given `q_online`, we identify its top-K hardest negatives `{k_i}` from the queue based on the current similarity `s_current = sim(q_online, k_i)`. We then define a historical similarity from the perspective of the momentum encoder: `s_historical = sim(q_momentum, k_i)`. The Momentum-based Trajectory Score (MTS) is the positive difference between these two scores:

    `MTS(q, k_i) = ReLU(s_current - s_historical)`

    A positive MTS signifies that the online encoder has learned to pull the query representation significantly closer to the negative `k_i` compared to the lagging momentum encoder. This directly captures the representation collapse characteristic of a false negative, providing a clean, instance-specific gradient signal.

3.  **Dynamically Debiased Contrastive Loss:** The standard InfoNCE loss is modified to incorporate the MTS. The similarity score for each of the top-K hard negatives `k_i` in the softmax denominator is adjusted as follows:

    `sim_adjusted(q, k_i) = s_current - О» * MTS(q, k_i)`

    Here, `О»` is a hyperparameter controlling the penalty strength. This adjustment dynamically down-weights the contribution of suspected false negatives, forcing the model to learn from more reliable hard negatives. The entire process requires only one extra forward pass through the momentum encoder for the query batch, which is computationally negligible.

**Novelty and Contributions:**

*   **State-Free and Efficient Trajectory Signal:** Unlike methods that require storing historical state per sample (e.g., an EMA or score variance), our approach is state-free. It derives the trajectory signal directly from the existing online and momentum encoders, adding minimal computational overhead and avoiding complex state management.
*   **Unambiguous Gradient-based Debiasing:** We use a direct proxy for the similarity score gradient (`s_current` vs. `s_historical`). This more effectively captures the representation collapse of false negatives compared to ambiguous signals like score variance (SRNS), which can misinterpret stable true hard negatives.
*   **Dynamic, Instance-Specific Correction:** Unlike the static, global prior in Debiased CL, our correction adapts at the instance-pair level based on the model's own learning dynamics, offering a more fine-grained debiasing.

**Experiments and Expected Impact:**

1.  **Quantitative Evaluation:** We will pre-train models on ImageNet-1K and evaluate on downstream tasks with high semantic overlap where false negatives are prevalent, such as fine-grained classification (CUB-200, Stanford Cars) and instance retrieval (In-Shop). We expect our method to significantly outperform baselines like MoCo, Debiased CL, and SRNS.
2.  **Ablation Studies:** We will conduct ablations on the penalty strength `О»` and the number of hard negatives considered (`K`). A crucial ablation with `О»=0` will isolate the performance gain attributable to our trajectory-based correction. We will also compare our MTS signal against an EMA-based approach to demonstrate the superiority of our state-free method.
3.  **Qualitative Analysis:** Using a dataset with known labels (e.g., CIFAR-100), we will track the average MTS for known true negatives versus known false negatives throughout training. We hypothesize that the MTS will be consistently and significantly higher for false negatives, thereby empirically validating our core assumption. We will also visualize the evolution of `s_current` and `s_historical` for selected anchor-negative pairs.

**Expected Impact:** This method is expected to yield superior representation quality by learning tighter and better-separated semantic clusters. By preventing model collapse due to false negatives, it will improve training stability and reduce sensitivity to hard-sampling hyperparameters. Furthermore, the MTS itself could serve as a novel online diagnostic tool for quantifying semantic ambiguity within datasets during self-supervised training.
